papers.title: P. succinatutens: Succinate Scavenging & Pathogen Inhibition
papers.abstract: P. succinatutens scavenges luminal succinate through its obligate succinate-to-propionate metabolic pathway, depriving opportunistic pathogens (Enterobacteriaceae) of a critical carbon source while simultaneously lowering luminal pH via propionate production. The methylmalonyl-CoA pathway is fully validated in vitro and in murine mono-colonization models (Level 3). Core enzymes (ScpA/ScpB/ScpC) show 100% amino acid sequence identity between two Phascolarctobacterium type strains. In porcine models, this function reaches only Level 1 (co-occurrence network inference). Critical evidence gap (Level 4 void): porcine-derived strain isolation and mono-colonization in weaned piglets is entirely absent.
papers.modelSystem: In vitro + Murine mono-colonization (L3); Porcine co-occurrence network (L1)
papers.keyLimitation: No porcine-derived strain isolated; no mono-colonization in piglets performed. Translational bottleneck: murine L3 to porcine L4.
papers.forwardJustification: P. succinatutens succinate-to-propionate pathway fully characterized in vitro. Methylmalonyl-CoA pathway enzymes (ScpA/ScpB/ScpC) 100% conserved. Murine mono-colonization validates causal propionate production.
papers.reverseJustification: No evidence for host control of P. succinatutens abundance or activity. Reverse pathway entirely uncharacterized in any model species.
papers.summary: Benchmark case for the translational gap. P. succinatutens succinate-to-propionate pathway is mechanistically validated at L3 in mice but only inferred from co-occurrence networks at L1 in pigs. Represents the critical evidence void at the murine-to-porcine translational interface. B12-dependence of the methylmalonyl-CoA pathway creates a functional intersection between the probiotic and Vitamin B12 nodes.

papers.title: P. succinatutens: Intestinal Barrier Maintenance via Propionate
papers.abstract: P. succinatutens maintains intestinal barrier integrity through propionate via a dual-pathway mechanism: (1) membrane pathway: GPR43/GPR41 activation rerouting signals through beta-arrestin and cAMP branches to block NF-kappaB nuclear translocation; (2) intracellular pathway: HDAC inhibition relieving epigenetic repression of tight junction proteins (ZO-1, occludin). In murine models, the complete causal chain of mono-colonization to propionate production to TLR4/NF-kappaB inhibition to barrier maintenance is established (Level 3). Two Level 2 studies in pigs independently validated propionate-mediated TLR4/NF-kappaB suppression and tight junction protein upregulation. Key limitation: porcine evidence validates propionate's barrier-protective effect but does not demonstrate that P. succinatutens produces sufficient propionate in situ for this effect.
papers.modelSystem: Murine mono-colonization (L3); Porcine dietary fiber fermentation models (L2)
papers.keyLimitation: Strain-specific causal validation missing in pigs: propionate's barrier effect validated, but P. succinatutens' in situ propionate production capacity in porcine gut not demonstrated.
papers.summary: Best-validated probiotic function node. Dual-pathway propionate signaling mechanism fully characterized. Translational gap: strain-specific propionate production quantification in porcine gut lumen and epithelium needed to advance from L2 to L3 in pigs.

papers.title: P. succinatutens: Lipid Deposition Reduction via MYC-Arginine-FOXO3a Axis
papers.abstract: P. succinatutens reduces host lipid deposition through the propionate-mediated MYC-arginine-FOXO3a molecular axis: HDAC inhibition leads to MYC transcriptional attenuation, derepression of glutamine transporters (SLC1A5/SLC38A5) enhances de novo arginine biosynthesis, and arginine accumulation suppresses PI3K/Akt/FOXO3a signaling to reduce adipogenic gene expression. Murine FMT and mono-colonization experiments have fully validated this pathway (Level 3). In porcine models, UCP1 knock-in pigs show negative correlation between fecal SCFAs and adipogenic gene expression (Level 1 association). The pathway is not P. succinatutens-specific in the porcine context. Functional divergence from P. faecium (which operates via TLR2) constitutes genus-level metabolic specialization.
papers.modelSystem: Murine FMT + mono-colonization (L3); Porcine UCP1 knock-in correlation (L1)
papers.keyLimitation: Pathway validated in mice only; porcine evidence is SCFA-adipogenic gene correlation, not P. succinatutens-specific. Pathway conservation across commercial pig breeds is speculative.
papers.summary: Demonstrates genus-level functional divergence (P. succinatutens vs P. faecium). MYC-arginine-FOXO3a axis is a potential target for lipid reduction in livestock. Porcine validation remains entirely at association level.

papers.title: Butyrate/SCFAs: Compartmentalized Co-Metabolism Node (Priority P1)
papers.abstract: Butyrate is the most extensively validated node in the compartmentalized co-metabolism network. Forward pathway (microbial metabolites to host epigenetics): Level 2b in vivo validation in porcine models. Xiong et al. (2016) landmark study demonstrated butyrate upregulates endogenous host defense peptides via HDAC inhibition in weaned piglets. Porcine ileal organoid experiments confirmed HDAC inhibition and barrier-enhancing effects. Tibetan pig studies showed butyrate promotes macrophage H4K31 crotonylation via mTORC1-HDAC3/8. Reverse pathway (host epigenetics to microbiota): only Level 1. Host gene regulatory networks (PRDM15, STAT1) and germ-free pig transcriptome data provide hypothesis-generating clues. Coupling: Level 0. Forward and reverse effects never simultaneously observed in a single experimental system. Measurement Depth: Level 0. All studies measured only a single compartment (luminal) at a single timepoint. The three-order-of-magnitude concentration difference between luminal (mM) and intracellular (muM) butyrate has not been incorporated into experimental design.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine in vivo (weaned piglets, Tibetan pigs) + ileal organoids
papers.keyLimitation: Forward pathway at L2b but reverse pathway at L1 and coupling at L0. The fundamental butyrate concentration gradient (lumen mM vs epithelium muM) has never been incorporated into experimental design. No study has simultaneously measured forward and reverse effects.
papers.summary: Most-validated co-metabolism node. Forward pathway well-characterized at L2b in pigs (HDAC inhibition, host defense peptides, crotonylation). But reverse pathway, coupling verification, and multi-compartment measurement all at L0-L1. Represents the core evidence asymmetry in the co-metabolism framework.

papers.title: Bile Acids: Host-Microbiome Signaling Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Bile acids are a porcine-validated core node. Forward pathway: Level 2b. Hyodeoxycholic acid (HDCA), the dominant porcine bile acid, enhances intestinal barrier function via FXR-PI3K/AKT pathway in weaned piglets, increasing Lactobacillus abundance from 5.28% to 37.97%. Neonatal piglets show bile acid-mediated epithelial proliferation and barrier maturation through TGR5 activation and FXR downregulation. Substantial species differences in bile acid pool composition (pigs: HDCA-dominant; mice: CA/DCA-dominant) limit murine evidence extrapolation. Reverse pathway: only Level 1. Whether host bile acid signaling alterations feed back to regulate microbial community composition has not been causally validated. Coupling: Level 0. FXR/TGR5 activation and microbial restructuring never simultaneously tracked. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Main evidence gap: concentration gradient data for bile acids at the lumen-epithelium interface are absent.
papers.keyLimitation: Species-specific bile acid composition (HDCA-dominant in pigs) renders murine data non-directly translatable. Reverse pathway causal validation void is the critical bottleneck for understanding bile acid-microbiota feedback circuits.
papers.summary: Porcine-validated forward pathway with species-specific bile acid (HDCA). Lactobacillus expansion from 5.3% to 38.0% is the strongest microbial community shift documented in any node. Reverse pathway (host-to-microbiome bile acid signaling) is the critical evidence gap. FXR-dependent antimicrobial peptide pathway recently identified as potential reverse mechanism.

papers.title: Tryptophan Metabolites: AhR Signaling Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Tryptophan metabolites are partially porcine-validated. Forward pathway: Level 2a (preliminary in vivo). Bacteroides thetaiotaomicron alleviates weaning diarrhea in piglets via tryptophan metabolism-AhR-Nrf2 signaling. Gut microbiota-mediated tryptophan metabolism suppresses pyroptosis via AhR signaling to maintain intestinal epithelial homeostasis in piglets. Dietary tryptophan supplementation modulates gut microbial composition and activates AhR signaling. Validations are based on specific bacterial strains or dietary interventions, not direct causal validation of tryptophan metabolites per se. Reverse pathway: Level 0. Tryptophan metabolite reverse regulatory role completely unexplored. Coupling: Level 0. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Core evidence gap: concentration gradients of tryptophan metabolites (indole, kynurenine) across lumen-epithelium-microenvironment compartments never simultaneously measured. Temporal asynchrony between AhR activation and microbial community shifts uncharacterized.
papers.keyLimitation: Validations are strain-specific (B. thetaiotaomicron) or dietary, not direct metabolite causal validation. AhR activation and pyroptosis suppression not linked causally in porcine gut in vivo. Reverse pathway entirely unexplored.
papers.summary: AhR signaling is the central mechanism linking tryptophan metabolites to intestinal homeostasis. Strain-specific (B. thetaiotaomicron) and dietary intervention evidence at L2a. Direct metabolite causal validation, reverse pathway, and multi-compartment measurement all at L0.

papers.title: Polyamines: Cross-Species Extrapolation Node (Priority P3)
papers.abstract: Polyamines are a cross-species extrapolated predicted node with no direct validation evidence at any level in porcine models. Forward pathway: only Level 1. All existing evidence derives from murine models and in vitro studies: polyamines regulate histone methylation and acetylation by promoting nucleosome condensation; polyamines and gut microbiota form bidirectional interactions maintaining intestinal homeostasis (including microbial growth and biofilm formation). None of these mechanisms have been experimentally verified in the porcine gut. Reverse pathway: Level 0. Coupling: Level 0. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Core evidence gaps: baseline concentration and spatial distribution data for polyamines in the porcine gut are entirely absent; polyamine biosynthetic capacity of porcine-derived strains uncharacterized; polyamine transporter expression patterns in porcine intestinal epithelium unknown.
papers.modelSystem: Murine models and in vitro only
papers.keyLimitation: No porcine data at any level. Baseline polyamine concentrations in porcine gut unknown. Porcine-derived polyamine-producing strains not isolated. Entirely dependent on cross-species extrapolation from murine evidence.
papers.summary: Weakest evidence base among all five nodes in porcine context. All evidence is murine-derived. Inclusion based on established mammalian host-microbe co-metabolic functions. Porcine functional relevance requires de novo establishment. First essential step: baseline polyamine concentration measurement in porcine gut compartments.

papers.title: Vitamin B12: One-Carbon Metabolism Node (Priority P3)
papers.abstract: Vitamin B12 has the weakest evidence base among all five nodes. Forward pathway: only Level 1 (association inference). B12 is a key cofactor in host one-carbon metabolism, participating in S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) synthesis to provide methyl donors for DNA methylation. This theoretical link is elaborated in reviews but direct causal validation between B12 concentrations and DNA methylation marks is absent in porcine models. In P. succinatutens' succinate-to-propionate pathway, B12 is the essential coenzyme for methylmalonyl-CoA mutase. B12 availability directly impacts propionate production efficiency, representing the functional intersection with Project 1. Reverse pathway: Level 0. Coupling: Level 0. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Core evidence gaps: B12 concentration distribution across porcine gut compartments unknown; B12-propionate metabolic coupling never validated in situ in porcine gut; causal effect of B12 on porcine intestinal epithelial DNA methylation untested.
papers.modelSystem: Theoretical/review-level only; no direct experimental validation
papers.keyLimitation: Weakest of all five nodes. Entirely at theoretical/review level. No experimental data on B12-DNA methylation link in porcine gut. B12-propionate coupling in situ completely untested. P. succinatutens B12 dependence is the only functional anchor in the co-metabolism network.
papers.summary: Weakest evidence node but critical for understanding one-carbon metabolism integration with microbial SCFA production. Functional intersection with P. succinatutens (methylmalonyl-CoA mutase requires B12) provides the mechanistic rationale for inclusion. Entirely a hypothesis awaiting experimental testing in porcine models.

papers.title: Propionate: Succinate-to-Propionate Pathway Node (Priority P1)
papers.abstract: Propionate is a major microbial SCFA in the porcine gut, produced via the succinate pathway (dominant in Bacteroidetes and Phascolarctobacterium) and the acrylate pathway (dominant in Firmicutes). Forward pathway (microbial propionate → host): Level 2b in pigs. Propionate activates GPR41/GPR43 in porcine intestinal epithelium, suppresses HDAC activity, and serves as a gluconeogenic substrate in the porcine liver. The P. succinatutens methylmalonyl-CoA pathway directly couples succinate consumption to propionate production, representing a B12-dependent metabolic intersection. Reverse pathway (host → microbial propionate): Level 1. Dietary fiber composition and host bile acid secretion patterns influence propionate-producing community structure, but direct host genetic control over propionate output is uncharacterized. Key evidence gap: propionate concentration gradients across the porcine gut epithelium have never been systematically mapped. Bidirectional coupling remains at Level 0.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine dietary intervention + in vitro bacterial cultures
papers.keyLimitation: Propionate concentration gradients across gut compartments unmapped. P. succinatutens in situ propionate production rate in porcine gut unknown. Reverse pathway entirely at association level. B12-propionate coupling never measured in vivo.
papers.summary: Propionate is the second-most abundant SCFA in the porcine hindgut and a core output of the succinate-to-propionate pathway. Wraps P. succinatutens metabolism (succinate→propionate), B12 cofactor requirement (methylmalonyl-CoA mutase), and host GPR41/43 signaling into a single integrated node. Forward pathway partially validated at L2b in pigs; reverse pathway and coupling entirely unexplored.
papers.forwardJustification: Propionate-GPR41/43 signaling validated in porcine epithelium. HDAC inhibition and gluconeogenic roles characterized. P. succinatutens pathway fully mapped in vitro.
papers.reverseJustification: No causal evidence for host control of propionate-producing community in pigs. Dietary fiber → propionate association established but no host genetic or immune mechanism characterized.

papers.title: Acetate: Cross-Feeding & Systemic Signaling Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Acetate is the most abundant SCFA in the porcine gut (typically 50-60% of total SCFAs) and the primary substrate for butyrate-producing bacteria via cross-feeding. Forward pathway (microbial acetate → host): Level 2a in pigs. Acetate crosses the gut epithelium via MCT1/SMCT1 transporters, enters the portal circulation, and reaches peripheral tissues at high concentrations (100-200 muM in human portal blood; porcine data limited). Acetate activates GPR43 on enteroendocrine L-cells, stimulates GLP-1/PYY secretion, and serves as a lipogenic substrate and epigenetic modifier (acetyl-CoA for histone acetylation). Reverse pathway (host → acetate producers): Level 1. Host diet composition and intestinal transit time influence acetogenic community structure, but host-driven feedback mechanisms are unexplored. Key evidence gap: the gut microbial acetate economy in pigs — production rates, cross-feeding fluxes, and epithelial transport kinetics — lacks systematic quantification.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine dietary fiber models (association level); Murine mono-colonization (causal)
papers.keyLimitation: Acetate cross-feeding network structure in porcine gut unknown. Portal acetate concentration data absent for pigs. Acetate contribution to host histone acetylation in porcine intestinal epithelium never quantified. Reverse pathway entirely at theoretical level.
papers.summary: Acetate is quantitatively dominant but mechanistically understudied in pigs. Its role as the primary cross-feeding substrate for butyrate producers makes it a critical upstream node in the SCFA network. Porcine-specific portal and peripheral acetate kinetics are a major data gap. Histone acetylation link offers a direct epigenetic readout for microbial acetate activity.
papers.forwardJustification: GPR43 activation and GLP-1/PYY secretion by acetate validated in murine models. Acetate-to-butyrate cross-feeding well-characterized in vitro. Porcine evidence limited to dietary fiber → acetate correlations.
papers.reverseJustification: No evidence for host-directed control of acetogenic community composition. Host diet as driver established; host immune/genetic feedback unexplored.

papers.title: Folate/B9: One-Carbon Metabolism & Methyl Donor Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Folate (vitamin B9) is a key microbial product in the gut that participates in host one-carbon metabolism alongside B12. Gut bacteria (especially Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus species) are net folate producers, contributing 10-30% of host folate requirements. Forward pathway (microbial folate → host): Level 1 in pigs. Microbial folate is absorbed in the colon via reduced folate carrier (RFC) and proton-coupled folate transporter (PCFT), contributing to host methyl donor pools (SAM cycle) and nucleotide synthesis. Bifidobacterium adolescentis folate production has been demonstrated in vitro and in murine models, but porcine in vivo evidence is absent. Reverse pathway (host → microbial folate): Level 1. Host dietary folate intake and the host folate absorption efficiency set the competitive landscape for microbial folate producers vs. auxotrophs. In pigs, dietary folic acid supplementation alters gut microbial composition but the mechanism (direct vs. indirect via host metabolism) is unclear. Coupling: Level 0. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Key gap: gut luminal folate concentrations and microbial folate production rates in pigs are completely unknown.
papers.modelSystem: Murine Bifidobacterium mono-association (L2a); Porcine dietary folic acid (L1 association)
papers.keyLimitation: Porcine gut luminal folate concentrations unknown. Microbial folate production rates in pig gut never measured. Bifidobacterium/Lactobacillus folate contribution to host methyl donor pool unquantified in any large animal model. B12-folate metabolic intersection in porcine gut unexplored.
papers.summary: Folate represents the microbial contribution to host one-carbon metabolism at the methyl donor level. Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus are the primary porcine-relevant folate producers. The B12-folate intersection in one-carbon metabolism is mechanistically established in human/murine systems but untested in pigs. Luminal concentration and production rate data are the prerequisite measurements.
papers.forwardJustification: Microbial folate production demonstrated in Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus in vitro. Colonic folate absorption via RFC/PCFT characterized in rodents. Porcine evidence: dietary folic acid changes gut microbiota composition at association level only.
papers.reverseJustification: Host dietary folate status influences competitive dynamics between folate producers and auxotrophs. Porcine dietary folic acid → microbiota shift observed but mechanistic pathway (direct microbial vs. host-mediated) not resolved.

papers.title: Vitamin A/Retinoic Acid: Immune-Microbiome Signaling Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Vitamin A and its active metabolite retinoic acid (RA) sit at the host-microbiome interface, regulating both host immunity and gut microbial ecology. Forward pathway (microbe-influenced RA → host): Level 2a in pigs. Gut bacteria influence host vitamin A metabolism through: (1) microbial production of retinaldehyde dehydrogenase (RALDH) cofactors that modulate host RA synthesis; (2) microbial SCFAs promoting dendritic cell RALDH expression and RA production. Porcine weaning stress decreases intestinal RALDH activity, correlating with microbial community disruption. Reverse pathway (host RA → microbiome): Level 2b in pigs. Retinoic acid directly regulates the gut microbiome through: (1) RA-induced epithelial IL-22/STAT3 signaling altering AMP secretion profiles; (2) RA-RAR signaling in B cells promoting IgA class switching, which shapes microbial composition; (3) RA modulation of Treg/Th17 balance influencing immune tolerance to commensals. This reverse pathway is better characterized than most nodes because the RA-RAR axis is well-studied. Coupling: Level 0 — RA effects on host immunity and microbial composition never tracked simultaneously in pigs. Measurement Depth: Level 0.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine weaning stress models (L2a); Murine RAR knockout + gnotobiotic (L3)
papers.keyLimitation: Simultaneous measurement of RA-dependent host immune activation AND microbial community shifts not performed in pigs. Porcine intestinal RA concentration and RALDH activity spatial mapping absent. The RA-RAR-IgA axis relevance to porcine gut commensal control unquantified.
papers.summary: Vitamin A/retinoic acid is the best-characterized REVERSE pathway node. The RA-RAR-IgA axis provides a mechanistically detailed host-to-microbiome signaling route. The forward pathway (microbes → host RA metabolism) is less characterized, creating an unusual reverse-dominant evidence asymmetry. Key AhR-RA crosstalk at the tryptophan-vitamin A metabolic intersection is unexplored in pigs.
papers.forwardJustification: Porcine weaning stress reduces intestinal RALDH activity (enzyme-level evidence). Microbial SCFA promotion of DC RALDH expression established in mice. Causal microbial → RA → host immune effect not demonstrated in pigs.
papers.reverseJustification: RA-RAR signaling in B cell IgA class switching well-characterized (molecular pathway). RA-IL-22-STAT3-AMP axis established. Porcine dietary vitamin A alters gut microbiota composition. Strongest reverse pathway evidence among all non-Bile Acid nodes at L2b.

papers.title: P. succinatutens: Porcine Gut Strain Isolation & Characterization Status
papers.abstract: Phascolarctobacterium succinatutens is an obligate succinate-consuming, propionate-producing bacterium in the family Acidaminococcaceae. It occupies a keystone metabolic niche in the porcine gut: its succinate-to-propionate pathway prevents luminal succinate accumulation (which would otherwise fuel Enterobacteriaceae blooms) while producing propionate for host signaling. Current isolation status: YIT 12066 and YIT 12067 were isolated from human feces (Yakult Central Institute, 2012). NO porcine-derived strain has been isolated to date. 16S rRNA gene surveys place Phascolarctobacterium at 1-5% relative abundance in healthy pig feces across diverse breeds (Yorkshire, Landrace, Duroc, Meishan). Metagenomic binning has recovered near-complete P. succinatutens genomes from pig fecal metagenomes. Core metabolic pathway (methylmalonyl-CoA) shows 95-100% amino acid sequence identity between human and porcine metagenome-assembled genomes. Key bottleneck: without a porcine-derived isolate, mono-colonization experiments in piglets cannot be performed, blocking L3→L4 translational advance.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine 16S/metagenomic surveys (L1); Human isolate in vitro (L2); Murine cross-colonization (L3)
papers.keyLimitation: No porcine-derived P. succinatutens isolate exists — the single most important bottleneck for advancing the translational pipeline from murine L3 to porcine L4. Without a pig-derived strain, causal validation in piglets is impossible.
papers.summary: P. succinatutens is the highest-priority strain for porcine isolation. Its succinate-to-propionate pathway is the mechanistic center of the co-metabolism framework. The human isolate (YIT 12066/12067) provides a genetic template, but porcine strain isolation is the prerequisite for advancing the entire Project 1 translational pipeline from L3 to L4. Core metabolic pathway is conserved but host-specific colonization factors remain unknown.
papers.forwardJustification: Succinate-to-propionate pathway fully characterized in human isolates. Methylmalonyl-CoA pathway enzymes (ScpA/ScpB/ScpC) 95-100% conserved in porcine MAGs. In vitro propionate production demonstrated. Host signaling effects inferred from propionate node.
papers.reverseJustification: Host dietary and immunological factors influencing P. succinatutens abundance in pig gut completely uncharacterized. Porcine intestinal factors driving niche specialization unknown. Dietary succinate availability is the only characterized host input.

papers.title: Lactobacillus: Porcine Gut Probiotic Ecology Node (Priority P1)
papers.abstract: Lactobacillus species (including Lactiplantibacillus, Limosilactobacillus, and Ligilactobacillus per reclassified taxonomy) are dominant small intestinal colonizers in pigs, reaching 10^7-10^9 CFU/g in the duodenum and jejunum. They are the most extensively studied porcine gut bacteria, with >200 published porcine feeding trials. Forward pathway (Lactobacillus → host): Level 2b in pigs. Multiple porcine trials demonstrate Lactobacillus-mediated pathogen inhibition (E. coli K88, Salmonella Typhimurium), intestinal barrier enhancement (ZO-1, occludin upregulation), and immune modulation (TLR2/TLR4 signaling, IgA promotion). However, strain-level specificity is high — effects observed with one strain (e.g., L. plantarum JDFM LP11) do not generalize. Reverse pathway (host → Lactobacillus): Level 2a in pigs. Host dietary fiber composition, weaning stress, and intestinal mucin glycosylation patterns all influence Lactobacillus community composition. Host antimicrobial peptide secretion (RegIII-gamma) selectively suppresses Lactobacillus in a porcine weaning model. Key gap: strain-level resolution is required — genus-level 16S data obscures functional divergence between species/strains.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine in vivo (weaned piglets, >200 trials) + porcine intestinal organoids
papers.keyLimitation: Genus-level data dominate; strain-level functional divergence is critical but under-characterized. Reclassified taxonomy (Lactobacillus → 25 genera, 2020) not reflected in most porcine literature. Strain-specific colonization factors and persistence in porcine gut poorly understood.
papers.summary: Lactobacillus is the most extensively studied porcine gut bacterium but suffers from nomenclatural fragmentation and inadequate strain-level resolution. Forward pathway evidence at L2b (barrier, immune, pathogen inhibition), reverse pathway at L2a (host AMPs and weaning stress). Represents the best-characterized bidirectional system among all strain nodes but lacks coupling (never measured simultaneously in same animals).
papers.forwardJustification: Extensive porcine trial literature: barrier enhancement (ZO-1/occludin), pathogen inhibition (E. coli, Salmonella), immune modulation (TLR2/4, IgA). Strain specificity high — effects not generalizable across Lactobacillus species.
papers.reverseJustification: Porcine weaning stress alters Lactobacillus community structure. Host RegIII-gamma AMP selectively suppresses Lactobacillus in piglets. Dietary fiber → Lactobacillus bloom established. Host mucin glycosylation influence demonstrated at L2a.

papers.title: Prevotella: Porcine Hindgut Dominance & Succinate Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Prevotella is the dominant Gram-negative genus in the porcine hindgut, often representing 15-30% of the fecal metagenome — substantially higher than in humans. Prevotella species are major succinate producers in the pig colon, making them the primary upstream metabolic partner for succinate-consuming bacteria (P. succinatutens). Forward pathway (Prevotella → host): Level 2a in pigs. Prevotella-dominated enterotypes are associated with higher luminal SCFA concentrations and improved feed efficiency in pigs. Prevotella copri produces succinate as a fermentation end-product, which is then cross-fed to propionate producers. Specific Prevotella species encode carbohydrate-active enzymes (CAZymes) targeting porcine dietary fibers (arabinoxylan, pectin). Reverse pathway (host → Prevotella): Level 2a. Host dietary carbohydrate composition is the dominant driver of Prevotella abundance. Porcine host genotype (MUC4, FUT1 polymorphisms) influences Prevotella colonization. Key gap: Prevotella-to-P. succinatutens succinate cross-feeding has never been directly measured in the porcine gut in situ. The succinate intermediate pool size and flux rate are unknown.
papers.keyLimitation: Prevotella-to-Phascolarctobacterium succinate cross-feeding never measured in situ in porcine gut. Succinate intermediate pool size unknown. Prevotella species-level functional diversity in pigs poorly characterized — genus-level data mask substantial metabolic heterogeneity.
papers.summary: Prevotella is the dominant succinate producer and primary metabolic partner for P. succinatutens in the porcine hindgut. The Prevotella→succinate→Phascolarctobacterium→propionate cascade is the central metabolite flow in the co-metabolism framework. Host dietary fiber composition is the dominant top-down driver of Prevotella abundance. Porcine-specific Prevotella CAZyme repertoire and succinate production kinetics are key unknowns.
papers.forwardJustification: Prevotella-dominated enterotype → higher SCFA and improved feed efficiency in multiple porcine cohorts. P. copri succinate production characterized in vitro. Porcine Prevotella CAZyme diversity documented at metagenomic level. Direct succinate flux to propionate producers not measured.
papers.reverseJustification: Host dietary carbohydrate composition is the dominant driver — well-established in multiple porcine dietary intervention trials. Host genotype (MUC4, FUT1) influence documented. Weaning transition shifts Prevotella abundance, driven by dietary change.

papers.title: Butyrate ameliorates maternal high-fat-diet-induced placental inflammation and offspring metabolic dysfunction via modulating gut microbiota in mice.
papers.abstract: Maternal high-fat diet (HFD) increases the risk of metabolic disorders in offspring. Placental inflammation acts as a critical mediator with poorly addressed etiology. Recently HFD-induced gut dysbiosis is demonstrated to be a key driver of systemic inflammation. Whether inflammatory signals triggered by HFD-induced gut dysbiosis are transmitted to the placenta via the maternal-fetal axis warrant further investigation. papers.title: Cadmium toxicity to the human gut microbiome varies depending on composition.
papers.abstract: Cadmium (Cd(II), hereafter Cd) is a toxic heavy metal that has detrimental effects on the gut microbiota. We investigated the effects of acute Cd exposure on human fecal microbiotas using 24-h in vitro cultures from 20 healthy adult donors. Regression analysis of butyrate production in the absence (-Cd) versus presence (+Cd) of Cd identified three categories of microbial responses: sensitive, intermediate, and resilient. Under Cd stress, sensitive microbiomes exhibited significant decreases in b

papers.title: Fasting primes small intestinal regeneration after damage via a microbiome-metabolite-chromatin axis.
papers.abstract: Fasting enhances small intestinal regeneration after radiation, but the contribution of the gut microbiome to this process remains uncharacterized. We identify Akkermansia muciniphila (AKK) as a key mediator of this response. AKK was enriched in fasted mice and its antibiotic depletion abrogated radioprotection, whereas reintroduction restored both organismal survival and intestinal integrity. papers.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

papers.title: From Dysbiosis to Diabetes: How Gut Microbiome Interventions Influence Type 2 Diabetes.
papers.abstract: Type 2 Diabetes (T2D) is a complex metabolic disorder associated with insulin resistance (IR), chronic low-grade inflammation, and dysregulated glucose metabolism. Increasing evidence suggests that gut microbiota imbalances, or dysbiosis, may play a key role in its development and progression. This review aims to critically evaluate the existing literature on the role of gut microbiome-targeted interventions, specifically prebiotics and probiotics, in the prevention and management of T2D. The re

papers.title: Rhubarb Extract Ameliorates Slow-Transit Constipation in Rats by Modulating Gut Microbiota and Bile Acid Homeostasis.
papers.abstract: Rhubarb, the dried rhizome and root of Rheum species, is a traditional Chinese medicine widely used for constipation relief. However, the mechanisms underlying its therapeutic effects on slow-transit constipation (STC) remain unclear.

papers.title: NIR-II Neuromodulation Combined with Metabolite-Mediated Immunoregulation for Accelerated Deeply Located Nerve Repair.
papers.abstract: Deep peripheral nerve regeneration is hindered by inflammatory infection, neurotrophic factor deficiency, and slow axonal growth kinetics. Although multifunctional nerve guidance conduits (NGCs) have been developed, achieving spatiotemporally precise neuromodulation within deeply located neural tissues remains a significant challenge. papers.title: Isolation and functional characterization of goose-derived lactic acid bacteria with growth-promoting and gut health benefits in Sanhua geese.
papers.abstract: Lactic acid bacteria (LAB) offer multiple probiotic benefits, yet host-specific strains for geese remain understudied. This study evaluated three goose-derived LAB strains, AK-GRW1, AK-GRW2, and AK-GRW3, for their effects on growth and intestinal health in geese. In vitro, AK-GRW1 exhibited the strongest antibacterial activity and acid production capacity, while AK-GRW2 showed the highest gastrointestinal tolerance. Additionally, all three strains remained sensitive to common antibiotics. In viv

papers.title: The milk fat globule membrane increases bacterial serotonin and exopolysaccharide production for maintenance of intestinal cell differentiation.
papers.abstract: Serotonin is a neurotransmitter that is primarily produced in the gut and is produced by lactic acid bacteria (LAB). It plays a crucial role in regulating mood and mental health disorders via the gut-brain axis using the enteric nervous system. Exopolysaccharides (EPS) are complex metabolic end products of probiotic LAB that contribute to health-promoting activities in the intestine. papers.title: Oat β-glucan reshapes gut microbiota to enhance glucose homeostasis via coordinated modulation of bile acid conjugation and succinate-dependent intestinal gluconeogenesis.
papers.abstract: Dietary fibers-induced gut microbiota changes influence diabetes through bile acid metabolism. Oat β-glucan is a beneficial dietary fiber that improves glucose metabolism, but its mechanism of regulating gut microbiota-mediated bile acid metabolism and exerting hypoglycemic effects remains unclear. Here, we found that oat β-glucan improved glucose intolerance and insulin resistance and promoted glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) secretion in obese mice. The enhanced production of secondary bile aci

papers.title: Towards ecological psychiatry: The herbicide glyphosate disrupts behavior through microbiota-gut-brain axis.
papers.abstract: Planetary and human health including mental health are closely interrelated. Increasing evidence also points to a role for the microbiota-gut-brain axis in maintaining optimum mental health. Emerging evidence raises concerns about the unintended toxicity of environmental exposure to xenobiotics, any substance foreign to the body, on the brain and behavior. Glyphosate is one of the most widely used active ingredients for herbicides for both agricultural and domestic applications. However, investi

papers.title: Intranasal stromal cell-derived factor-1α mitigates parkinsonian deficits via dual modulation of neuroinflammation and gut microbiota in MPTP-induced models.
papers.abstract: Parkinson's disease (PD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by dopaminergic neuronal loss and neuroinflammation, with emerging evidence implicating gut-brain axis dysregulation in its pathogenesis. Stromal cell-derived factor-1α (SDF-1α), a chemokine with neuroprotective properties, remains underexplored as a therapeutic agent for PD. This study investigates the efficacy of intranasal SDF-1α administration in mitigating motor deficits, gastrointestinal (GI) dysfunction, an

papers.title: The interplay between gut microbiota and Alzheimer's disease: mechanistic insights from dysbiosis to disease modulation.
papers.abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a chronic, progressive, neurodegenerative condition marked by memory loss and cognitive decline. It is characterized by neuropathological features such as amyloid plaque accumulation, neurofibrillary tangles of tau protein, and inflammatory changes in the brain. Recent research emphasizes how gut microbes influence the onset and progression of AD primarily through the gut-brain connection, a bidirectional communication system. papers.title: Characterization and determination of the immunomodulatory activity of intestinal microbiota microorganisms isolated from free-range Cornu aspersum snails in olive groves before and after aestivation.
papers.abstract: The present study investigated the impact of aestivation of free-range snails on the intestinal microbiota and its potential to protect the host from infection by pathogenic bacteria. Bacterial strains were isolated just before and at the end of aestivation from the gut of free-range Cornu aspersum aspersum snails from the island of Crete (Greece). Α total of 31 BA (Before Aestivation) and 25 EA (End Aestivation) isolates, were tested for their in vitro probiotic properties. Principal component

papers.title: Effects of probiotics intervention on sperm parameters: A systematic review on literature.
papers.abstract: Background: Male infertility is a critical global health issue, often linked to oxidative stress and inflammation. Probiotics, known for modulating gut health, have been proposed as a potential intervention for improving male reproductive health. However, the evidence remains fragmented, necessitating a systematic review to consolidate findings. Objective: This study systematically evaluates the effects of probiotic interventions on sperm parameters, including count, motility, morphology, and DN

papers.title: Ketogenic diet alleviates acute radiation-induced intestinal injury through JAK2/STAT3/RORγt/IL-17A signaling pathway via gut microbiome.
papers.abstract: Emerging evidence suggests dietary interventions regulate inflammatory signaling through gut microbiome modulation, yet their therapeutic potential in radiation-induced intestinal injury (RIII) remains underexplored. This study demonstrates that ketogenic diet (KD), a high-fat and low-carbohydrate dietary regimen, exerts protective effects against RIII through dual mechanisms involving microbial regulation and inflammatory pathway inhibition. Using high-salt diet (HSD) as a dietary control, KD s

papers.title: Bifidobacterium animalis reshapes the bile acid pool and prevents neonatal jaundice: a clinical microbiome study from correlation to causation.
papers.abstract: Neonatal jaundice (NJ) affects 60-80% of neonates, yet the underlying microbial mechanisms remain elucidated, despite known links between gut dysbiosis and bilirubin and bile acid (BA) metabolism. Through two-stage shotgun metagenomic-metabolomic analysis of 150 fecal samples from 120 neonates, we identified key taxa linked to bile acid (BA) metabolism in moderate-to-severe NJ. papers.title: Author Correction: Gut commensal Christensenella minuta modulates host metabolism via acylated secondary bile acids.
The microbiota-mitochondria axis: linking metabolic dysfunction to neurodegeneration.
papers.abstract: The interplay between gut microbiota and mitochondria represents a dynamic relationship that profoundly impacts host physiology, ranging from maintaining intestinal homeostasis to regulating systemic metabolic and neurological functions. Microbial metabolites such as short-chain-fatty-acids, bile acids, and amino acid derivatives serve as pivotal modulators of mitochondrial bioenergetics, oxidative stress management, and fission-fusion processes. papers.title: Chronic Exposure to NaAsO2 Induces Renal Fibrosis by Modulating Gut Microbiota-Mediated AhR/NLRP3 Inflammasome Signaling Pathway.
papers.abstract: Arsenic is an environmental contaminant with potent renal toxicity. Accumulating evidence has indicated that gut microbiota dysbiosis plays an important role in kidney disease. However, the role of gut microbiota in arsenic-exposure-induced renal injury remains unclear. In our study, chronic exposure to NaAsO2 and fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) from NaAsO2-exposed mice increased intestinal permeability and elevated renal indoxyl sulfate (IS), a key metabolite of gut microbiota, which pro
papers.journal: Journal of agricultural and food chemistry

papers.title: In situ sol-gel-sol transformation formed by sodium alginate realizes folic acid-modified chitosan nanoparticles to deliver hops β-acids for colorectal cancer therapy.
papers.abstract: Oral administration can deliver targeted nanoparticles directly to colorectal cancer (CRC) lesions, bypassing systemic circulation. However, orally administered nanoparticles may become physically entrapped in the small intestine, compromising therapeutic efficacy. To overcome this limitation, we designed and prepared folic acid-modified, CRC cell-targeting nanoparticles (FA/CS/TPP/β-acids) and incorporated them into a sodium alginate hydrogel (Alg/FA/CS/TPP/β-acids). papers.title: Lactobacillus gasseri alleviates type 2 diabetes via modulation of gut microbiota-host metabolic networks.
papers.abstract: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) is a multifactorial metabolic disorder characterized by insulin resistance, dyslipidemia and inflammation. Although probiotics have shown potential in improving glycemic control in T2DM, the systemic mechanisms underlying their therapeutic effects remain largely unclear.

papers.title: A Common Indoor Pollutant, 1,4-Dichlorobenzene, Induces Colonic Inflammation via Vitamin D Receptor-Reg3γ-Gut Microbiota Axis.
papers.abstract: 1,4-Dichlorobenzene (1,4-DCB), a widely used household insect repellent and fungicide, has been epidemiologically linked to ulcerative colitis, yet its potential mechanisms remain poorly understood. In this study, juvenile male mice developed colonic inflammation following exposure to 1,4-DCB at doses of 0.03-3 mg/kg/day, a range corresponding to the human estimated daily intake (EDI) and relevant internal exposure levels in adolescents. This was evidenced by increased fecal moisture (by 12.22%-

papers.title: A Lactobacillus plantarum vaccine candidate with dendritic cell targeting and lysosome escape properities provides efficient protection against necrotic enteritis in broiler chickens.
papers.abstract: Necrotic enteritis (NE) caused by Clostridium perfringens (C.perfringen) type G strains remains a critical threat to the global poultry industry, particularly with the restricted use of antibiotics. The core virulence factor Necrotic Enteritis B-like Toxin (NetB), along with the adhesion factors Fimbrial protein A (FimA) and Fructose-1,6-bisphosphate aldolase (Fba), are key targets for multivalent vaccination. In this study, we developed a surface-displayed Lactobacillus plantarum (L. plantarum)

papers.title: Differences in gut microbiota composition between ischemic stroke patients and healthy individuals.
papers.abstract: Ischemic stroke is a leading cause of mortality and disability worldwide. Emerging evidence suggests that the gut microbiota, acting through the gut-brain axis, may play a critical role in stroke pathophysiology. Dysbiosis, an imbalance of gut microbiota, has been associated with neuroinflammation and poor stroke outcomes. This study aimed to compare gut microbiota composition in ischemic stroke patients versus healthy controls to explore potential microbial changes linked to stroke.

papers.title: Gut-brain axis modulation by fecal microbiota transplantation improves dual-organ injury after cerebral ischemia-reperfusion via Caspase-8 dependent inhibition of necroptosis.
papers.abstract: The pathological features of cerebral ischemia-reperfusion (CIR) include necroptosis activation. This study investigated how healthy fecal microbiota transplantation (H-FMT) improves CIR and intestinal barrier damage. Rats subjected to middle cerebral artery occlusion and reperfusion (MCAO/R) were treated with H-FMT and/or a Cysteine-aspartic acid protease-8 (Caspase-8) inhibitor. Survival and body weight were monitored throughout the experiment. Neurological function, tissue damage, inflammator

papers.title: Alginate-based biofilm-assisted encapsulation of a Co-culture of Bifidobacterium longum DSM20219 and Bacillus subtilis SOM8 enhances resistance to gastrointestinal stress.
papers.abstract: Oral probiotic formulations are increasingly explored for supporting gastrointestinal health, restoring gut microbiome balance, and providing non-invasive alternatives to injections or transplants. However, delivering oxygen-sensitive probiotics orally remains challenging because gastric acidity, bile salts, digestive enzymes, and aerobic storage can substantially reduce bacterial viability. Sodium alginate is widely used for probiotic encapsulation because it forms mild, cell-compatible hydroge

papers.title: IL-17A deficiency in HLA-DR3 transgenic mice enriches beneficial Prevotella species in gut to promote Tregs and reduce CNS autoimmunity.
papers.abstract: Human Leukocyte Antigen (HLA) class-II genes, particularly HLA-DR2 and HLA-DR3, and the gut microbiota are intricately linked to the pathobiology of multiple sclerosis (MS) through their ability to regulate host immunity, a critical factor in disease pathogenesis. An imbalance between anti-inflammatory CD4+ Tregs and pro-inflammatory IL-17A-secreting CD4+ Th17 cells is thought to drive disease. However, a key unresolved question is whether HLA-class II-restricted CD4+IL-17A cells can influence T

papers.title: Metabolic Profiling of the Gut-Brain Axis in a Murine Model of Ulcerative Colitis with Raman Spectroscopy.
papers.abstract: The gut-brain axis (GBA) enables a bidirectional crosstalk between the enteric and central nervous systems and plays a critical role in inflammatory diseases of the gut. This study is the first demonstration of the utility of Raman spectroscopy (RS) in capturing a snapshot of the biochemical changes that modulate the GBA and identify vibrational bands that are evidence of metabolites present in a murine model of ulcerative colitis. Acute colitis was induced in mice by administering dextran sulfa

papers.title: Multiomic insights into fungal polylactic acid degradation: Metabolic adaptation and hydrolytic mechanisms of Sporobolomyces pararoseus.
papers.abstract: Polylactic acid (PLA), a biodegradable polyester from renewable resources, is a sustainable alternative to petrochemical plastics. However, its environmental degradation is inefficient naturally, requiring specific microbial activities. While bacterial PLA-degrading mechanisms are well documented, fungal degrading systems-particularly their molecular mechanisms-are underexplored.We isolated Sporobolomyces pararoseus ZRQ01 from the gut microbiota of PLA-fed mealworms. papers.title: L-Glutamine Plus L-Glutamic Acid Enhances Antioxidant Status and Ammonia Toxicity Resilience, Upregulates Interleukin IL-10 Gene, and Improves Gut Microbiota and Survival in Juvenile Nile Tilapia.
papers.abstract: Glutamine (Gln) and glutamic acid (Glu) are the most abundant free amino acids (AAs) in the fish body. Although classified as non-essential AAs, their supplementation can be a strategy to optimize the growth performance and health of fish. This study aimed to investigate the effects of dietary Gln and Glu blend on growth performance, biochemical parameters, gut microbiota composition, short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) production, digestive enzyme activity, histomorphometry, and liver mRNA levels o
papers.journal: Journal of animal physiology and animal nutrition

papers.title: Deep metagenomics uncovers functional adaptations and pathogenic risks in the gut microbiome of Antarctic fur seals (Arctocephalus gazella).
papers.abstract: The Antarctic fur seal (Arctocephalus gazella) plays a key role in the Antarctic marine ecosystem by regulating krill, fish, and cephalopod populations through selective foraging, promoting Southern Ocean productivity via excretion, and influencing coastal island ecosystems during breeding season. Despite the importance of the gut microbiota in reflecting diet, health, and environmental adaptation, the gut microbiome of the Antarctic fur seal remains poorly characterized. To address this gap and

papers.title: Gut microbiota dysbiosis in chronic liver disease: Mechanisms driving hepatocellular carcinoma progression and therapeutic implications of Chinese medicine.
papers.abstract: Chronic liver disease represents a major global public health challenge, and its malignant progression to hepatocellular carcinoma is the leading cause of death among affected patients. Gut microbiota dysbiosis is a critical driver of this process. As the central hub of the "gut-liver axis," the gut microbiota, when disrupted, compromises the integrity of the intestinal mucosal barrier, promoting the translocation of microbial metabolites, such as lipopolysaccharides and aberrant secondary bile

papers.title: Intermittent fasting ameliorates Sjögren's syndrome-related dry eye through a preponderant bile acid-Akkermansia homeostasis establishment.
papers.abstract: Intermittent fasting (IF) has emerged as a promising immunometabolic intervention, yet its therapeutic value in Sjögren's syndrome (SS)-related dry eye remains unclear. In this study, IF robustly improved tear secretion and corneal integrity in SS mice and markedly reshaped the gut ecosystem. papers.title: The microbiome-epigenome axis: Regulation of host genome function across development and disease.
papers.abstract: The gut microbiome is increasingly recognized as a metabolically active regulator of host gene expression, translating environmental exposures-particularly diet-into epigenetic signals that shape development, immunity, metabolism, and disease susceptibility. This narrative review synthesizes current evidence for a microbiome-epigenome axis in which microbial metabolites and regulatory molecules modulate DNA methylation, histone modifications, non-coding RNA networks, RNA epitranscriptomic marks,

papers.title: Exploring the multi-barrier repair effects of Codonopsis pilosula water extract in improving rhubarb-induced intestinal dysfunction in mice based on the tryptophan metabolism-AhR pathway.
papers.abstract: The intestine is the core digestive and absorptive organ and the largest immune barrier. Intestinal dysfunction impairs physiological function, but current treatments remain suboptimal because of its complex etiologies. This study investigated whether Codonopsis pilosula water extract (CPWE) repairs intestinal mucosal barrier damage in mice and elucidated the underlying mechanisms.

papers.title: Differential Effects of Prenatal Depression and Anxiety on Infant Sleep: Dual-Pathway Mechanisms Involving the HPA Axis and the Gut-Brain Axis.
papers.abstract: Prenatal psychological distress is associated with adverse offspring outcomes, including infant sleep disturbances and altered gut microbiota, yet the mediating roles of neonatal gut microbiome and tryptophan metabolism remain underexplored.

papers.abstract: This study aimed to develop a novel pH-sensitive core-shell delivery system to improve probiotics' viability during simulated digestion and storage. A 10-strain premix of Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium was encapsulated within a pregelatinized starch-based gel core, while alginate/pectin (Al/P) gel was employed as the shell using a 3D food printing (3DFOODP) process. papers.title: Postbiotic Lactobacillus sakei CVL-001 promotes mucosal tolerance via NOD2-dependent programming of tolerogenic CD11c+ antigen-presenting cells.
papers.abstract: Postbiotics offer improved safety and stability over live probiotics for modulating intestinal immunity, yet the receptor-mediated mechanisms underlying mucosal tolerance remain unclear. Nucleotide-binding oligomerization domain 2 (NOD2), a cytosolic sensor of bacterial cell wall components and a major genetic risk factor for inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), may link microbial signals to regulatory immune programs. papers.title: Peptidoglycan from Lactiplantibacillus plantarum YC Alleviates Hyperuricemia via the "Microbiota-Gut-Kidney" Axis: Structural Characterization and Metabolic Regulation.
papers.abstract: Hyperuricemia (HUA) is a prevalent metabolic disorder necessitating safe and effective interventions. Although probiotics demonstrate potential in HUA management, the specific contributions of their cellular components remain unexplored. In this study, peptidoglycan (PGN) isolated from Lactiplantibacillus plantarum YC was characterized as an A1γ-type chemotype (l-Ala-d-iGln-mDAP-d-Ala) via amino acid analysis, FTIR, NMR, and MALDI-TOF MS. papers.journal: Journal of agricultural and food chemistry

papers.title: Integrated analysis of gut microbiota, serum metabolomics, and proteomics reveals novel associations with clinical symptoms in patients with cerebral infarction.
papers.abstract: Cerebral infarction (CI) is a major cause of adult disability and mortality worldwide. Mounting evidence supports the critical role of the gut-brain axis in cerebrovascular disease progression. This study aimed to characterize the alterations in gut microbiota, serum metabolome, and serum proteome in patients with CI, and to identify multi-omics signatures associated with clinical symptoms.

papers.title: The human gut microbe Eubacterium limosum utilizes flavodoxin over ferredoxin for lactate metabolism.

papers.title: Gut bacterial metabolite imidazole propionate potentiates Alzheimer's disease pathology.
papers.abstract: The gut microbiome modulates metabolic and neurovascular processes implicated in Alzheimer's disease and related dementias (ADRD), but the underlying mechanisms remain unclear. Here, we identify the bacterial metabolite imidazole propionate (ImP) as a modifier of ADRD pathology. In a cohort of 1196 cognitively unimpaired adults, higher plasma ImP levels were associated with lower preclinical cognitive scores and biomarkers of ADRD, both cross-sectionally and longitudinally. papers.title: The gut microbial metabolite 3-indolepropionic acid as a functional neuroprotective agent against intracerebral hemorrhage: integrating epidemiological screening with in vivo validation.
papers.abstract: Intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) is a severe stroke subtype with limited therapeutic options. Emerging evidence highlights the diet-gut-brain axis in neurological outcomes, yet the specific metabolic mediators remain elusive. This study integrated epidemiological, genetic, and experimental approaches to investigate the potential neuroprotective role of gut-derived metabolites in ICH. papers.title: Tong-Xie-Yao-Fang ameliorates IBS-D: Potential role of Alistipes finegoldii-associated gut tryptophan indole metabolism.
papers.abstract: Irritable bowel syndrome with diarrhea (IBS-D) is a prevalent chronic gastrointestinal condition characterized by visceral hypersensitivity, low-grade mucosal inflammation, and impaired epithelial barrier integrity. Current therapies remain limited, highlighting the need for more alternative strategies. Tong-Xie-Yao-Fang (TXYF), a classical Chinese herbal formula, has shown clinical efficacy in IBS-D, however, the mechanisms underlying its therapeutic effects remain unclear.

papers.title: Effects of an infant formula containing a whey protein concentrate on feeding tolerance and markers of intestinal immune defense in Chinese infants.
papers.abstract: Human milk (HM) bioactive components can have immune modulatory functions, impact the gut microbiome, and may result in functional benefits when added to infant formula (IF). In this single-arm, prospective, intervention study, we tested the effectiveness of an IF with a whey protein concentrate co-enriched in α-lactalbumin, milk fat globule membrane (MFGM), and Sn-2 palmitate resulting in protein and lipid profiles observed in HM. papers.title: Effects of tryptophan combined with Limosilactobacillus reuteri M6 on the growth performance and intestinal barrier function of heat-stressed broilers.
papers.abstract: This study investigated the effects of Tryptophan (Trp) combined with Limosilactobacillus reuteri M6 (L. reuteri M6) on growth performance and intestinal barrier function of broilers subjected to heat stress (HS). A total of 250 21-day-old male broilers (676.24 ± 1.43 g) were randomly assigned to five groups (5 replicates of 10 birds each). papers.title: Effects of fermented Arundo donax juice on growth performance, immunocompetence, antioxidant capacity, and intestinal digestive enzyme activity in Arbor Acres broilers.
papers.abstract: In this study, we used lactic acid bacteria to ferment the byproduct of Arundo donax papermaking, Arundo donax juice (ADJ), to obtain fermented Arundo donax juice (FADJ). We aimed to investigate the effects of FADJ on the growth performance, immunocompetence, antioxidant capacity, and intestinal digestive enzyme activity of broilers. papers.title: Efficacy of combination of Lacticaseibacillus rhamnosus and Bifidobacterium bifidum probiotic strains against mixed infections of bovine Rotavirus A and Cryptosporidium parvum induced diarrhea by modulating host immune response in mouse model.
papers.abstract: Neonatal calf diarrhea is a serious health issue in dairy calves with significant economic losses. The immature gut immunity and the inadequate passive transfer of immunity in neonates favour the transmission and replication of the bovine rotavirus A (RVA) and the apicomplexan Cryptosporidium spp., and others, causing significant morbidity and mortality. papers.title: Preventive effect of heat-killed Lactiplantibacillus plantarum FM8 in an ovalbumin-induced food allergy murine model.
papers.abstract: Paraprobiotics, the non-viable microbial cells with health benefits, have gained interest as candidates for preventing food allergies owing to their safety and stability. Heat-killed Lactiplantibacillus plantarum FM8 (FM8) has been reported to induce interleukin (IL)-10 production in dendritic cells in vitro; however, its in vivo efficacy as a standalone intervention remains unexplored. papers.journal: Allergy, asthma, and clinical immunology : official journal of the Canadian Society of Allergy and Clinical Immunology

papers.title: Maternal butyrate administration ameliorates fetal fatty liver and maternal metabolic alterations related to maternal obesity.
papers.abstract: Obesity negatively impacts maternal and fetal metabolism, leading to the programming of metabolic disturbances in the offspring. We have previously reported numerous maternal, fetal and offspring alterations in a rat model of obesity. papers.title: Study on the therapeutic effects of the primary active ingredients in cinnamon oil for constipation-predominant irritable bowel syndrome and their regulatory effects on the 5-HT signaling pathway and gut microbiota.
papers.abstract: Cinnamon oil exhibits therapeutic potential against constipation-predominant irritable bowel syndrome (IBS-C). This study aimed to evaluate the efficacy and mechanisms of its main components-cinnamaldehyde (CAL), cinnamyl alcohol (COL), and trans-cinnamic acid (TCA)-in IBS-C. papers.title: Novel genetic insights into causal effects of depression on non-alcoholic fatty liver diseases partially mediated by gut microbiota.
papers.abstract: Depression and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) are increasingly recognized as interconnected disorders, yet the causal mechanisms linking them remain unclear. Using univariable and multivariable Mendelian randomization (MR), we demonstrated that depression causally increased the risk of NAFLD (P = 8.473 × 10-8, OR = 1.944, 95% CI: 1.524 to 2.479), independent of major metabolic confounders. papers.title: Integrated analyses of metagenomics, metabolomics and culture-based assays reveal functional roles of gut microbiota in Felidae.
papers.abstract: The functional roles of gut microbiota in carnivores remain poorly understood. Here, we integrated metagenomics, metabolomics, proteomics and culture-based functional assays to characterize metabolic potential of gut microbiota across 14 captive Felidae species. Comparative metagenomics analysis revealed that the Felidae gut microbiome is distinct from that of non-Felidae and reflects carnivorous dietary patterns. papers.title: Effects of Heat-Moisture Treatment on the Physicochemical Properties, Structural Characteristics, and Anti-Ulcerative Colitis Activity of Yam Starch.
papers.abstract: Yam starch (YS) alleviates ulcerative colitis (UC), but whether heat-moisture treatment (HMT) enhances this effect remains unclear. This study examined the impact of HMT on the structural features of yam starch and yam flour (YF) and their UC-mitigating capacity. The results showed that HMT increased the resistant starch (RS) content in YF by forming starch-lipid complexes and, in YS, it enhanced the RS content through recrystallization. papers.title: Gut microbiota profiles across intrinsic capacity strata in community-dwelling older adults using full-length 16S rRNA sequencing.
papers.abstract: Intrinsic capacity (IC), introduced by the World Health Organization, provides a multidimensional framework for evaluating functional aging across locomotion, cognition, sensory, psychological, and vitality domains. However, gut microbial features associated with IC among community-dwelling older adults remain incompletely understood. papers.title: Effect of Probiotic Supplementation in Older Individuals with Mild Cognitive Impairment and Alzheimer's Disease: A Randomized, Placebo-Controlled, Triple-Blind Clinical Trial.
papers.abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is the most common type of dementia in older adults and often precedes mild cognitive impairment (MCI). These conditions are associated with biological alterations involving chronic inflammation, neuronal dysfunction, and genomic instability. In parallel, growing evidence has suggested a role for the gut-brain axis in cognitive aging, and probiotics have been investigated as a potential strategy to modulate inflammatory and neurotrophic pathways. papers.title: Role of Toll-like receptors and oral-gut-brain axis in neurodegenerative and neuropsychiatric disorders.
papers.abstract: The oral-gut-brain axis is a path connecting the gastrointestinal tract and the central nervous system (CNS). The gut microbiota influences the immune system, metabolism, and nerve cells through the production of neurotransmitters and microbial metabolites that can cross the blood-brain barrier (BBB). papers.title: Blueberry anthocyanin-pretreated Akkermansia muciniphila enhances probiotic efficacy against metabolic syndrome via gut microbiota remodeling and hepatic signaling modulation.
papers.abstract: Targeting the gut microbiota is a promising strategy for treating metabolic syndrome (MetS). Previous studies have shown that dietary components, including polyphenols and dietary fibers, can modulate the abundance of Akkermansia muciniphila (AKK). However, whether dietary compounds can enhance the probiotic function of AKK through a pretreatment strategy remains largely unexplored. papers.title: Effects of health-functional products on gut microbiota-derived short-chain fatty acids production: an ex vivo and pilot human study.
papers.abstract: This study investigated the effects of health-functional products on short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) production using an ex vivo gut simulation platform alongside a human pilot study. papers.title: Extraction method shapes the structure and fermentation behavior of white kidney bean episperm polysaccharides in gut microbiota of the elderly.
papers.abstract: Aging is often accompanied by gut microbiota dysbiosis. Dietary supplementation with polysaccharides offers a promising, cost-effective approach to restore microbial balance and support healthy aging. This study investigated structural characteristics and in vitro fermentation behavior of white kidney bean episperm polysaccharides extracted using ultrasound-assisted acid (AEP), enzyme (EEP), and chelator (CEP) methods. papers.title: Divergent Colorectal Cancer Risks Following Metabolic Bariatric Surgery: Anatomical Remodeling and the Genotoxic Microenvironment.
papers.abstract: Metabolic bariatric surgery (MBS) reduces overall cancer incidence, yet colorectal cancer (CRC) risk diverges by procedure. Roux-en-Y gastric bypass (RYGB) has been associated with increased long-term CRC risk (HR 1.55 at 10-14 years), whereas sleeve gastrectomy (SG) shows no equivalent elevation, though shorter follow-up (mean 4.5 vs. 8.5 years) precludes definitive conclusions. papers.title: Exploring the effects of sugarcane bagasse extracts on human gut microbiota via fecal batch fermentation.
papers.abstract: This study investigated the effects of four sugarcane bagasse (SB) extracts, namely crude carbohydrates (CC), crude polyphenols (CP), partially purified carbohydrates (PPC), and partially purified polyphenols (PPP), on the gut microbiome using an in vitro human gut model. Both CP and PPC had strong modulatory effects on the gut microbiota. papers.journal: Journal of food science and technology

papers.title: Gut microbiota-derived indole-3-propionic acid alleviates endoplasmic reticulum stress by regulating FMO2 in MASLD.
papers.abstract: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) has become a global health challenge for which there are currently no approved drugs. Indole-3-propionic acid (IPA), as one of the primary metabolites of gut microbiota, can influence the development of liver diseases through the gut-liver axis, but how it contributes to liver diseases remains unclear. The present study was conducted to explore the possible molecular mechanisms of IPA in the development of MASLD.

papers.title: Oligosaccharide prebiotics in functional foods and therapeutics: innovations and challenges.
papers.abstract: Oligosaccharide prebiotics, such as inulin, fructooligosaccharides (FOS), and galactooligosaccharides (GOS), have demonstrated significant effects on gut microbiota and host health across in vitro, animal, and clinical studies. These studies consistently report an increase in beneficial bacteria, particularly Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus, leading to higher production of short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) such as acetate, propionate, and butyrate.
